text: The surgeon begins the procedure by opening the gastro-colic omentum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins the procedure by opening the gastro-colic omentum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac, providing access to the posterior aspect of the stomach and pancreas.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac, revealing the anatomical space behind the stomach and in front of the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to further open the gastrocolic omentum, bringing down the colon to expose the pancreas and the lesser sac.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to further open the gastrocolic omentum, bringing down the colon to expose the pancreas and the lesser sac.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum towards the distal stomach, progressing in the direction of the pylorus, using robotic assistance.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum towards the distal stomach, progressing in the direction of the pylorus, using robotic assistance.
The surgeon observes significant adhesions between the stomach and pancreas, likely resulting from a previous perforation during an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) procedure.
The surgeon encounters significant adhesions between the stomach and pancreas, likely resulting from a previous perforation during an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) procedure.
The surgeon enters the inflammatory cavity located between the pancreas and the stomach, draining fluid from the cavity, which is a result of a complication from the EUS.
The surgeon enters the inflammatory cavity located between the pancreas and the stomach, draining the fluid within. This cavity is a result of a complication from the EUS.
The surgical field reveals necrotic tissue in the area between the pancreas and the stomach.
The surgeon observes necrotic tissue within the inflammatory cavity located between the pancreas and the stomach.
The surgeon focuses on mobilizing the colon, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon focuses on mobilizing the colon, manipulating it to achieve better access or positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing its anatomical position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing its anatomical position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully lowers the colon, ensuring it is moved without causing injury, while maintaining awareness of its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully locates the colon and begins to gently mobilize it downward, ensuring no injury occurs during the process.
The surgeon navigates through inflamed tissue, visibly encountering areas of inflammation during the procedure.
The surgical field reveals significant inflammation, with visibly swollen and reddened tissue.
The surgeon navigates through inflamed tissue, carefully dissecting to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar device to carefully dissect and remove adhesions, revealing the pancreas amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes the pancreas, making it visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar forceps to carefully dissect and remove adhesions around the pancreas.
The surgeon exposes the DJ flexure at the level of the ligament of Treitz, revealing its anatomical position in relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the DJ flexure at the level of the ligament of Treitz, revealing its anatomical position amidst surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs careful lasso adhesion using cold scissors, meticulously cutting through the adhesions around the pancreas.
The surgeon performs a careful lasso adhesion using cold scissors, navigating through inflamed tissue.
The surgeon inspects the colon, visualizing the middle colic artery, while ensuring careful handling to avoid injury.
The surgeon inspects the colon, examining its surface and surrounding structures for any abnormalities or adhesions.
The middle colic artery is visualized, appearing prominently within the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to remove adhesions until the duodenal flexure is fully separated from the inferior border of the pancreas. The inferior mesenteric vein is visible at this level, entering the splenic vein.
The surgeon continues to remove adhesions until the duodenal flexure is fully separated from the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon locates the inferior mesenteric vein, which is observed entering the splenic vein.
The surgeon continues dissection beneath the pancreas, moving towards the retropancreatic plane. In a normal pancreas, this plane appears undisturbed, requiring minimal dissection to access.
The surgeon continues dissection beneath the pancreas, progressing towards the retropancreatic plane, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon approaches the retropancreatic plane, which typically requires minimal dissection in a normal pancreas, indicating the plane is undisturbed.
The surgeon uses a tip-up fenestrated instrument to lift the pancreas, elevating it from the surrounding tissue and exposing the underlying structures.
The surgeon uses a tip-up fenestrated instrument to lift the pancreas, revealing underlying structures and facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic ultrasound probe to locate the tumor within the pancreas and assess the need for further dissection to achieve a negative margin.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic ultrasound probe to visually confirm the location of the tumor within the pancreas.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the pancreas, aiming to achieve a negative margin by carefully separating tissue planes around the organ.

The surgeon directs attention to freeing the stomach from the superior border of the pancreas, using a vessel sealer to dissect the inflammatory wall between these structures, allowing access to the splenic artery.
The surgeon focuses on freeing the stomach from the superior border of the pancreas, preparing to dissect the splenic artery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and remove the inflammatory wall between the stomach and the pancreas, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The robotic system provides a ten-time magnified view, enhancing the visualization of the surgical field as the surgeon carefully dissects around the pancreas and adjacent structures.
The robotic system provides ten-time magnification for enhanced visualization, aiding the surgeon in performing a careful dissection of the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a robotic suction irrigator to carefully lyse adhesions while simultaneously suctioning fluids. The colon is gently retracted to avoid damage during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic suction irrigator to carefully release adhesions while simultaneously suctioning fluids from the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls the colon, likely to improve visibility and access to the surgical area.
The surgeon uses heat electrocautery to dissect along a clearly visible tissue plane, separating structures with precision.
The surgeon uses heat electrocautery to dissect along a clearly visible tissue plane, separating structures with precision.
The surgeon re-enters the inflammatory cavity, using suction to remove fluid from the area, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The surgeon enters the inflammatory cavity, utilizing robotic assistance for precise visualization and dissection.
The surgeon uses a robotic suction irrigator to remove fluid from the inflammatory cavity, ensuring the area is clear of excess fluid.
After safely repositioning the colon, the surgeon focuses on creating a retropancreatic tunnel, carefully maneuvering around the pancreas to establish a clear pathway.
The surgeon safely lowers the colon and begins forming a retropancreatic tunnel, focusing on the anatomical area behind the pancreas.
The surgeon identifies the inferior pancreatic artery arising from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), visible within the surgical field.
The inferior pancreatic artery is visible, arising from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).
The surgeon applies two clips to the artery and then transects it, completing the separation of the vessel.
The surgeon applies two clips to the artery and then transects it, completing the division of the vessel.
The surgeon attempts to expose the splenic artery located on the superior aspect of the pancreas, carefully maneuvering to reveal the artery's position.
The surgeon attempts to expose the splenic artery located on the superior surface of the pancreas, carefully maneuvering around the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon exposes the superior border of the pancreas, revealing a thick inflammatory peel covering the surface.
The surgeon observes the superior border of the pancreas, which is covered by a thick inflammatory peel.
The surgeon identifies the artery superior to the pancreas, ensuring it is not mistaken for the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon identifies an artery superior to the pancreas, ensuring it is not mistaken for the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects the artery, progressing towards the pancreas to confirm its pathway, ensuring it does not mistakenly lead to the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects the artery along the superior border of the pancreas, aiming to expose the common hepatic artery while avoiding misidentification.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pancreas, confirming the pathway of the artery as it enters the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon clamps the artery, observing for changes in liver color to ensure blood flow is not compromised. The spleen is inspected, showing signs of ischemia.
The surgeon clamps the artery to ensure blood flow to the liver is not compromised, observing a change in liver color. The spleen is inspected for signs of ischemia.
The surgeon performs dissection beneath the splenic artery, attempting to encircle the artery with a vessel loop, visible above the pancreas.
The surgeon performs dissection beneath the artery, attempting to encircle it with a vessel loop, while the artery is positioned above the superior border of the pancreas.
The surgeon completes the dissection around the artery and places a vessel loop around it, ensuring secure encirclement.
The surgeon completes the dissection around the artery and places a vessel loop around it, ensuring the artery is encircled securely.
The surgeon places a vessel loop around the artery, completing the encirclement initiated in previous steps.
The surgeon attempts dissection towards the common hepatic artery, encountering difficulty due to severe inflammation in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon attempts dissection towards the common hepatic artery, focusing on the area surrounding the artery.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in dissecting around the common hepatic artery due to severe inflammation, which is visibly affecting the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein located posterior to the pancreas, carefully exposing it for further dissection.
The surgeon begins to identify the splenic vein located posterior to the pancreas, carefully navigating the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon forms a tunnel between the splenic vein and the pancreas, creating a visible space between these structures.
The surgeon forms a tunnel between the splenic vein and the pancreas, creating a visible passage through the inflamed tissue.
The surgeon passes a vessel loop around the artery, completing the encirclement beneath the artery following the dissection.
The surgeon passes a vessel loop around the identified splenic vein, which is visible behind the pancreas amidst severe inflammation.
The surgeon visually confirms that the vessel looped is the splenic artery, ensuring it is not mistakenly looped around the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon visually confirms the identity of the looped vessel, ensuring it is the splenic artery and not the common hepatic artery, amidst the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the pancreas, visually confirming that the vessel dives into the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the pancreas, confirming visually that the vessel dives into the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon uses a robotic vascular stapler to transect the confirmed splenic artery after verifying its identity.
The surgeon removes the vessel loop from around the splenic artery, which had been previously looped and confirmed.
The surgeon removes the vessel loop from around the previously identified splenic artery, which was looped to confirm its identity.
The surgeon clips and transects the splenic vein, preparing for a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy.
The surgeon clips the splenic vein and then transects it, completing the separation of the vein from surrounding tissues.
A vessel loop is passed behind the pancreas to elevate it, facilitating the use of a green load 45 mm stapler to transect the pancreas.
A vessel loop is passed behind the pancreas to elevate it, facilitating the stapling of the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a green load of 45mm staplers to transect a section of tissue, likely part of the pancreas or associated vessels.

The surgeon clamps the pancreas using a stapler with smart technology, adjusting the clamp multiple times to achieve the appropriate thickness. The clamp is held for three minutes before firing, successfully transecting the pancreas. The vessel loop remains on the specimen side, avoiding additional resection.
The surgeon clamps the pancreas using a stapler equipped with smart technology, preparing for transection.
The surgeon attempts to clamp the pancreas, adjusting the clamp multiple times to achieve the appropriate thickness before holding it for three minutes and firing the stapler.
The surgeon transects the pancreas using a stapler, completing the separation of the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon prepares to fire the stapler on the pancreas, with the vessel loop still in place on the specimen side, aiding in elevation.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas in a medial to lateral fashion using a vessel sealer and Maryland bipolar forceps. The short gastric vessels are taken down, and the entire stomach is mobilized, completing the steps initially not performed.
The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas in a medial to lateral direction using a vessel sealer and a Maryland bipolar forceps, carefully separating the pancreas from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the takedown of the short gastric vessels and mobilizes the entire stomach, which was not performed initially.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the pancreas using a medial to lateral approach, while simultaneously taking down the splenic attachment to the diaphragm.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the pancreas using a medial to lateral approach, further freeing the organ from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon detaches the splenic attachment from the diaphragm, completing the separation of these structures.

The surgeon inspects the stomach for injuries by submerging it in saline, with the NG tube connected to continuous oxygen. The stomach appears intact with no visible injuries. The colon is also inspected and confirmed to be intact. A drain is placed in the left upper quadrant, and the pancreatic staple is not reinforced.

The surgeon inspects the stomach for injuries after specimen removal, submerging it in saline while connected to an NG tube with continuous oxygen. The stomach appears intact with no visible injuries.
The surgeon inspects the colon, visually confirming that there is no injury present.
A drain is placed in the left upper quadrant after confirming the colon is intact.
The surgeon observes the pancreatic staple, noting that it is not reinforced.
